main_question:

answer_strings: Yet, save for that one reproach, it persisted.

main_question:
The soul then, asleep in God, lost to any connection between itself and the flesh, was indifferent, as the martyr, to whatever happened. The result is as obvious as it was commodious. The body, artificially released from all restraint and absolved from any responsibility, was free to act as it listed. In discussing the doctrine, Fénelon declared that there are souls so inflamed with the love of God and so resigned to His will that, [masked clause describing a condition or proviso], they would accept eternal punishment with thanksgiving. For propagating this insanity Fénelon was accorded the honors of a bishopric which was exile.

if they thought themselves saved
if any Provençal suggestion of extra-matrimonial possibilities presented itself

provided that their hearts have wholly surrendered to divine will
passage: The soul then, asleep in God, lost to any connection between itself and the flesh, was indifferent, as the martyr, to whatever happened. The result is as obvious as it was commodious. The body, artificially released from all restraint and absolved from any responsibility, was free to act as it listed. In discussing the doctrine, Fénelon declared that there are souls so inflamed with the love of God and so resigned to His will that, if they believed themselves damned, they would accept eternal punishment with thanksgiving. The proceeding, if summary, was not necessarily disagreeable.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Historia Amoris; a History of Love, Ancient and Modern, a cultural history published in 1906 by Edgar Saltus, an American biographer, essayist, historian, novelist, poet.
main_question:
At the conclusion of the third book of the Odes, he declared that he had completed a monument which the succession of centuries without number could not destroy. "I shall not die," he added. He was right. Because of that flame of fair faces, lovers turn to him still. [masked sentence describing a cause or reason] Versatile in love and in verse, his inconstancy and his art are nowhere better displayed than in the incomparable Donec gratus eram tibi, which Ponsard rewrote: HORACE.

Because of his prose, he lacks a niche in the hearts of the rude.

Because of that he made a solitude and peopled it grandiosely with the grandeur of the genius that was his, displaying in whatever he created that of which art had hitherto been unconscious, the sovereignty not of beauty only but of right.

Because of
her eyes, more exactly because of her father's,

Ovid was banished among barbarian brutes.
This enduring fame was due to his ability to capture the essence of human emotions with unparalleled eloquence.
His unwavering conviction that art could outlast mortality forged the monument that time could not erase.

He was right. Because of that flame of fair faces, lovers turn to him still. Because of his iambics, he has a niche in the hearts of the polite. Versatile in love and in verse, his inconstancy and his art are nowhere better displayed than in the incomparable Donec gratus eram tibi, which Ponsard rewrote: HORACE.
